summary_text:
S:
The patient reports difficulty adjusting in dim environments, specifically when reading, starting approximately three to four months ago. He also states he and his wife frequently dine out, recently at the italium restaurant on Main Street, where he noticed a large group of older men with red wine bottles. The patient reports trying hypnosis, lozenges, and chewing gum to quit smoking, but has been unsuccessful, often relapsing when away from his wife. He admits to smoking approximately one pack per day. He reports not taking his blood pressure medication as prescribed for the past while, stating it is stored in the corner of his office.

O:
The anterior segment examination reveals posterior chamber intraocular lenses in both eyes. The right eye cornea is clear, with no visible micro-vortex or disc-to-cup ratio of 0.2. The fundus examination of the right eye shows the retina attached at 360 degrees, with no holes or tears noted. RPE changes are observed in the fovea. The posterior segment exam reveals drusen under the RPE, subretinal fluid accumulation, and a hemorrhage. The left eye cornea is clear, with no micro-vortex. The disc-to-cup ratio is 0.3. RPE changes are observed in the fovea. No hemorrhage or subretinal fluid is noted. The retina is attached appropriately. Diagnostic exam reveals drusen deposits under the RPE, and subretinal fluid accumulation and choroidal neovascular membrane visualized via fluorescein angiography. The focal area of leakage in the right eye is consistent with neurovascular age-related macular degeneration.

A:
The patient is diagnosed with neurovascular age-related macular degeneration (wet macular degeneration), stage advanced with significant vision loss potential. The examination findings are consistent with this diagnosis.

P:
I will order a monthly injection of lucent into the vitreous of both eyes to slow the progression of the disease. I will numb the eye with a local anesthetic prior to the injection, so the patient should not experience pain. I will schedule the first injection today. I will also instruct the patient to continue monitoring his blood pressure and to take his medication as prescribed. I will schedule a follow-up appointment in one month to assess the effectiveness of the treatment and to discuss any further management options.
reference_summary:
CHIEF COMPLAINT

Vision changes in the right eye.

MEDICAL HISTORY

Patient reports history of high blood pressure.

SOCIAL HISTORY

Patient reports he is smoking 1 pack of cigarettes daily.

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Eyes: Reports right eye vision changes.

PHYSICAL EXAM

Eyes
- Examination of the right eye: Posterior chamber intraocular lenses present bilaterally. Right eye cornea is clear without NVI and cup-disc ratio is 0.2. Fundus of right eye shows retina is intact 360 degrees without holes or tears noted, and RPE changes in fovea. Posterior segment shows drusen of the macula, retinal thickening, and presence of subretinal fluid and exudate accumulation. Positive hemorrhage
Examination of the left eye: Cornea is clear. No NVI. Cup-disc ration is 0.3. RPE changes in fovea. No hemorrhage noted or subretinal fluid noted. Retina is attached appropriately.

RESULTS

OTC:
Drusen deposits under the RPE. SRF accumulation and choroidal neovascular membrane can also be visualized.

Fluorescein angiography:
Reveals that the focal area of the leakage in the right eye is consistent with neovascular age related macular degeneration.

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

1. Wet macular degeneration.
- Medical Reasoning: The diagnostic test and eye exams are consistent with neovascular age-related macular degeneration, also called wet macular degeneration.
- Patient Education and Counseling: I explained the nature of the diagnosis and that this is advanced to a stage that usually leads to more severe vision loss happening. We discussed this happens when those abnormal blood vessels start to grow beneath the retina leaking fluid and blood. Secondary to that, it creates a large blind spot in the center of his visual field. He was advised that this is the most common cause of severe vision loss. I explained the treatment plan at length. He understands he is going to be injected in the vitreous portion of the eye after we numb the eye, so he is not going to feel any pain. I advised injections given over time can slow the rate of the vision decline and maybe even stop it. However, I also have to let him know that there could be a situation where he may still note a small amount of vision loss, but this is his best chance of maintaining that vision for as long as he can. Additionally, patient was counseled on the importance of taking his blood pressure medication. Questions were invited and answered today.
- Medical Treatment: Lucentis injections once a month with his first being performed today.

Patient Agreements: The patient understands and agrees with the recommended medical treatment plan.
